symbols: pointing gesture — the prophetic act of proclamation, directing attention toward the coming Messiah
branches — the 'Branch' (Tsemach) prophecy of Zechariah 3:8 and 6:12, the messianic title for the coming Davidic king
green skin — organic life, renewal, and the prophetic vision of cosmic restoration; possibly evoking the verdant imagery of messianic promise
arched niche — the prophetic office as a sacred threshold between heaven and earth, echoing Sistine Chapel prophet niches
dark curtain — the veil between the seen and unseen, the mystery that prophets are given to penetrate
description: A digitally rendered figure of a seated, nearly nude man is shown within a stone arched niche. The figure has green-tinted skin, a bald head, and a grey beard. He sits on a red platform with one leg forward, his right hand raised with index finger pointing upward in a declamatory gesture. Dry, barren branches emerge from around and behind his body, wrapping partially around his limbs. A dark blue-grey draped curtain fills the niche behind him. The architectural frame is rendered in warm stone tones. Text at the bottom reads 'Saul Zanolari, SZ CHAPEL MMXIII.MMXV, 2015, detail (Zaccaria).
theological_description: This contemporary digital rendering of the Prophet Zechariah draws on the Renaissance tradition of monumental prophet figures, most notably Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling, where Old Testament prophets are enthroned in architectural niches as heralds of the coming Christ. Zechariah (Hebrew: 'God remembers') was a post-exilic prophet whose visions are among the most explicitly messianic in the Old Testament. His prophecies include the 'Branch' who will rebuild the Temple (Zech 6:12-13), the pierced one whom Israel will mourn (Zech 12:10 -- cited in John 19:37 at the Crucifixion), and the king who comes 'humble and riding on a donkey' (Zech 9:9 -- fulfilled in Christ's triumphal entry, Matt 21:4-5). The barren branches entwining the figure may allude to the messianic 'Branch' (Tsemach) title that Zechariah proclaims, or to the vision of the lampstand and two olive trees (Zech 4:1-14) representing the anointed ones who stand before the Lord. The prophet's pointing finger directs the viewer forward in salvation history -- past the exile, past the rebuilt Temple, to the Incarnation that fulfills all prophetic longing. The green flesh may evoke the organic vitality of prophetic utterance: the Word of God that is alive, growing, and generative even when spoken through mortal flesh.

scripture_refs.reason: 'Rejoice greatly, O daughter of Zion! Your king comes to you, righteous and having salvation, humble and riding on a donkey' -- Zechariah's most famous messianic prophecy, fulfilled in Christ's Palm Sunday entry.
'The man whose name is the Branch... will build the temple of the LORD and will be clothed with majesty' -- the Branch prophecy visually evoked by the branches surrounding the figure.
'They will look on me, the one they have pierced' -- a prophecy applied to Christ crucified in John 19:37 and Revelation 1:7.
The vision of the golden lampstand and two olive trees -- the anointed ones who serve the Lord, a vision of priestly and royal messiahship.
'I am going to bring my servant, the Branch' -- the messianic title linking the dry branches in the image to eschatological hope.
Matthew's citation of Zechariah 9:9 at Christ's triumphal entry, demonstrating the prophet's direct Christological fulfillment.
John cites Zechariah 12:10 at the piercing of Christ's side, linking the prophet's vision to Calvary.
'Every eye will see him, even those who pierced him' -- the Apocalyptic echo of Zechariah's prophecy of the pierced one.
